metadata.title: Why Cancer Cells Are Harder to Kill With the Drug That Should Kill Them Best
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL #1F6F5C = apoptotic signal present / death pathway active / circuit working; CRIMSON #BF3339 = sabotage point / resistance mechanism / selection pressure; GOLD = editor's pen, once (B12 only)
metadata.note: EXCLUSIONS (honored absolutely): no extrinsic pathway, no necroptosis/pyroptosis/ferroptosis, no venetoclax mechanism (separate card 24), no p53 transcriptional detail (PUMA/NOXA absent from narration), no IAP biochemistry beyond XIAP naming.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Wonder register (voices/wonder/VOICE.md + MEDHAVY.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the MEDHAVY outro from AUTHOR.MD :: Medhavy.com
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: Three relapses. Three rounds of chemotherapy. Each time, DNA damage. Each time, a new population survived.
By the third relapse, these leukemic B cells have one defining feature: they refuse to die.

props.headline: By the third relapse, these leukemic B cells have one defining…

viz.prompt_seed: bone marrow biopsy microscopy slide showing dense clusters of abnormal lymphocytes, clinical pathology, editorial treatment

beats.narration_text: Here's what's interesting: chemotherapy delivers enough DNA damage to trigger apoptosis in normal cells. The same damage reaches these leukemic cells. But the death program never executes. Why does the signal arrive and then stop?

Here is why apoptosis should work. DNA damage triggers a signal. That signal tips the balance at the mitochondria — a pore opens. Cytochrome c escapes. Caspases execute death. That is the intrinsic pathway, intact in these cells.

viz.note: Intrinsic pathway spine: damage -> signal -> BCL-2 balance -> MOMP -> caspases -> death. All teal, flowing left to right. This is the intact, working circuit.

beats.narration_text: In a healthy cell, that circuit completes. The leukemic cell runs the same circuit. The signal arrives. Here's where it gets strange — something is intercepting it before it finishes.

viz.note: Same circuit, now with a crimson STOP block appearing at the BCL-2 balance node. Signal arrives left; nothing exits right.

beats.narration_text: Five sabotage points. Not one — five. Each a separate defense. Each one selected by a prior chemotherapy exposure.

First sabotage: BCL-2 overexpression. BCL-2 grabs BAX — the protein that would punch a pore through the mitochondrial membrane — and holds it immobilized. No free BAX means no pore. No pore means no death.

viz.note: Intrinsic circuit. A crimson BLOCKER labeled 'BCL-2 HIGH' appears at the MOMP node, snapping into place. BAX held, no pore.

beats.narration_text: Second sabotage: BAX is lost entirely. Some cells don't just restrain BAX — they delete it. No effector protein exists at all. Even if BCL-2 is defeated, there is nothing left to open the membrane.

viz.note: Second crimson BLOCKER labeled 'BAX LOST' appears at effector position. Circuit now has two blocks.

beats.narration_text: Third sabotage: IAPs — inhibitor of apoptosis proteins. Even when MOMP does occur, caspases must still activate to execute death. XIAP blocks them directly at the execution stage. The pore opens and still nothing happens.

viz.note: Third crimson BLOCKER labeled 'XIAP' appears at caspase node. Three blocks on circuit now.

beats.narration_text: Fourth sabotage: PI3K-AKT survival signaling. AKT phosphorylates BAD — a pro-apoptotic protein — and inactivates it. The pressure on BCL-2 drops. The guardian's grip on BAX gets easier to hold.

viz.note: Fourth crimson BLOCKER labeled 'AKT-BAD' appears at BH3-only sensor node. Four blocks.

beats.narration_text: Fifth sabotage: p53 mutation. p53 is the hub that converts DNA damage into an apoptotic signal. Lose p53 and the damage sensor fires — but the message never reaches the mitochondria. The upstream alarm rings in an empty room.

viz.note: Fifth crimson BLOCKER labeled 'p53 MUT' appears at p53/sensor node. All five blocks now on circuit. The complete sabotage map.

beats.narration_text: The interesting part is this: five redundant defenses mean a drug that clears one of them leaves four intact. The death signal still cannot push through. Redundancy is the architecture.

viz.note: Full five-block circuit held. Pause. GOLD signal arrow attempts to cross from left; stops at first block. Emphasizes redundancy.

beats.narration_text: Here is how selection assembled it. A leukemic clone meets treatment one. Most cells die. The BCL-2-high cells survive — not because they mutated in response, but because they were already there.

viz.note: Many teal dots (cells); crimson selection pressure; most disappear; one BCL-2-high dot remains enlarged. Label: 'BCL-2 HIGH — SELECTED.' Illustrative.

beats.narration_text: Treatment two hits the BCL-2-high clone. Now cells that have also lost BAX survive. Treatment three: IAP levels rise in the survivors. Each exposure narrows the population and hardens what remains.

viz.note: Staircase continues. Step 2 adds 'BAX LOST'; Step 3 adds 'XIAP UP'. Each step narrows the surviving clone but hardens it. Illustrative.

beats.narration_text: The death signal was arriving the whole time. The cancer spent three treatment cycles building a five-layer wall against it — one layer per exposure. CANCER BIOLOGY.
